children.text: All information discussed or disclosed in the course of any mediation shall be strictly confidential, and the Executive Director shall notify each person participating in the mediation of the confidentiality requirement and of the sanctions applicable to any person who violates the confidentiality requirement.

Except as provided in subsections (c), (d), and (e), all proceedings and deliberations of hearing officers and the Board, including any related records, shall be confidential. This subsection shall not apply to proceedings under

children.tail: , but shall apply to the deliberations of hearing officers and the Board under that section. The Executive Director shall notify each person participating in a proceeding or deliberation to which this subsection applies of the requirements of this subsection and of the sanctions applicable to any person who violates the requirements of this subsection.

children.text: Release of records for judicial action
The records of hearing officers and the Board may be made public if required for the purpose of judicial review under
Automatic referral to Congressional Ethics Committee of dispositions of claims involving Members of Congress and senior staff
Upon the final disposition under this subchapter (as described in paragraph (6)) of a claim alleging a violation described in

children.tail: committed personally by a Member of the House of Representatives (including a Delegate or Resident Commissioner to the Congress) or a Senator, or by a senior staff of the House of Representatives or Senate, the Executive Director shall refer the claim to—

children.text: the Committee on Ethics of the House of Representatives, in the case of a Member or senior staff of the House; or
the Select Committee on Ethics of the Senate, in the case of a Senator or senior staff of the Senate.

If the Executive Director refers a claim to a Committee under paragraph (1), the Executive Director shall provide the Committee with access to the records of any preliminary reviews, hearings, or decisions of the hearing officers and the Board under this chapter, and any information relating to an award or settlement paid, in response to such claim.
Review by Senate ethics committee of settlements of certain claims
After the receipt of a settlement agreement for a claim that includes an allegation of a violation described in

children.tail: committed personally by a Senator, the Select Committee on Ethics of the Senate shall—

children.text: not later than 90 days after that receipt, review the settlement agreement;
determine whether an investigation of the claim is warranted; and
if the Select Committee determines, after the investigation, that the claim that resulted in the settlement involved an actual violation described in

children.tail: committed personally by the Senator, then the Select Committee shall notify the Executive Director to request the reimbursement described in
and include the settlement in the report required by section 1381(

children.text: If a Committee to which a claim is referred under paragraph (1) issues a report with respect to the claim, the Committee shall ensure that the report does not directly disclose the identity or position of the individual who filed the claim.
Committee authority to protect identity of a claimant

If a Committee to which a claim is referred under paragraph (1) issues a report as described in paragraph (4) concerning a Member of the House of Representatives (including a Delegate or Resident Commissioner to the Congress) or a Senator, or a senior staff of the House of Representatives or Senate, the Committee may make an appropriate redaction to the information or data included in the report if the Chairman and Vice Chairman of the Committee reach agreement—
that including the information or data considered for redaction may lead to the unintentional disclosure of the identity or position of a claimant; and
on the precise information or data to be redacted.

The report including any such redaction shall note each redaction and include a statement that the redaction was made solely for the purpose of avoiding such an unintentional disclosure of the identity or position of a claimant.
The Committee making a redaction in accordance with this paragraph shall retain a copy of the report, without a redaction.
In this subsection, the “final disposition” of a claim means any of the following:
An order or agreement to pay an award or settlement, including an agreement reached pursuant to mediation under
A final decision of a hearing officer under

children.tail: that is no longer subject to review by the Board under

children.text: A final decision of the Board under

children.tail: that is no longer subject to appeal to the United States Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit under

children.text: A final decision in a civil action under

children.tail: that is no longer subject to appeal.

children.text: In this subsection, the term “senior staff” means any individual who, at the time a violation occurred, was required to file a report under title I of the Ethics in Government Act of 1978 (5 U.S.C. App. 101 et seq.).

children.tail: See References in Text note below.

children.text: A final decision entered under section 1405(g) or 1406(e) of this title shall be made public if it is in favor of the complaining covered employee, or in favor of the charging party under

children.tail: , or if the decision reverses a decision of a hearing officer which had been in favor of the covered employee or charging party. The Board may make public any other decision at its discretion.

children.text: Nothing in this section may be construed to prohibit a covered employee from disclosing the factual allegations underlying the covered employee’s claim, or to prohibit an employing office from disclosing the factual allegations underlying the employing office’s defense to the claim, in the course of any proceeding under this subchapter.
This chapter, referred to in subsec. (d)(2), was in the original “this Act”, meaning

children.tail: , which is classified principally to this chapter. For complete classification of this Act to the Code, see Short Title note set out under

children.text: The Ethics in Government Act of 1978, referred to in subsec. (d)(7), is

, (2), redesignated subsec. (b) as (a) and struck out former subsec. (a). Prior to amendment, text of subsec. (a) read as follows: “All counseling shall be strictly confidential, except that the Office and a covered employee may agree to notify the employing office of the allegations.”
, (3), redesignated subsec. (c) as (b) and substituted “subsections (c), (d), and (e)” for “subsections (d), (e), and (f)”. Former subsec. (b) redesignated (a).
, substituted “All information discussed or disclosed in the course of any mediation” for “All mediation”.
, redesignated subsecs. (d) and (e) as (c) and (d), respectively. Former subsec. (c) redesignated (b).
, redesignated subsec. (f) as (e). Former subsec. (e) redesignated (d).

, amended subsec. (e) generally, substituting provisions relating to automatic referral to congressional ethics committee of dispositions of claims involving Members of Congress and senior staff for provisions relating to access by committees of Congress.
